title: Quiz: Hardware Noise Mitigation
description: Test your understanding of hardware techniques for reducing noise in quantum systems

questions.question: Why should microwave attenuation be placed at the warmest effective cryogenic stage?

options.text: Attenuators work better at higher temperatures
It reduces thermal noise at its source, replacing warm noise with cold noise
Cold attenuators generate too much heat
The signal needs to be stronger at cold stages

questions.explanation: Attenuating at warm stages removes thermal noise where it is generated, replacing it with noise at the local (colder) temperature. For example, 20 dB attenuation at 4K reduces 4K thermal noise by 100x and replaces it with noise at 4K temperature, which is then attenuated further by downstream stages.

questions.question: For effective magnetic shielding of superconducting qubits, which arrangement is most effective?

options.text: Single thick layer of mu-metal
Multiple thin layers of mu-metal with gaps between them
Superconducting shield only
Aluminum enclosure

questions.explanation: Multiple thin layers of high-permeability material provide better shielding than a single thick layer because the shielding factors multiply. Gaps between layers prevent magnetic saturation and allow each layer to work independently. The innermost layer is often superconducting for perfect diamagnetic shielding.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Eccosorb filters in a cryogenic system?

options.text: To provide electrical grounding
To absorb and thermalize infrared and stray microwave radiation
To increase signal power
To reduce mechanical vibrations

questions.explanation: Eccosorb is a lossy material that absorbs electromagnetic radiation across a broad frequency range. In cryogenic systems, it absorbs IR radiation that would otherwise break Cooper pairs and create quasiparticles. It also absorbs stray microwave radiation that could excite qubits.

questions.question: Reducing the participation ratio of the electric field in lossy interface regions is more practical than improving the material quality of those interfaces.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! While improving material quality (reducing tan_delta) is challenging due to the fundamental nature of interface defects, reducing participation ratios through geometric design is often more achievable. 3D cavities and suspended structures move the electric field away from surfaces, reducing their impact on Q.

questions.question: Why is infrared filtering important even when the cryostat reaches base temperature?

options.text: IR radiation heats the mixing chamber
IR photons from warmer stages can break Cooper pairs even at mK temperatures
IR radiation interferes with qubit readout
IR filtering improves vacuum quality

questions.explanation: IR photons from warmer stages (even 4K) have enough energy (~meV) to break Cooper pairs in the superconductor (Al gap ~0.4 meV). A single IR photon can create multiple quasiparticles that cause T1 degradation. This occurs even at mK base temperatures because the photons originate from warmer stages.

questions.question: Which of the following are effective strategies for reducing TLS-related losses? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Using tantalum instead of aluminum for capacitor pads
Substrate cleaning with piranha solution or HF
Using 3D cavity designs to move fields away from surfaces
Operating at higher microwave power to saturate TLS

questions.explanation: Effective TLS reduction strategies include: (a) tantalum has a better quality oxide than aluminum, (b) surface cleaning removes contaminants that form TLS, and (c) 3D designs reduce participation ratios at lossy interfaces. While TLS saturation at high power (d) can reduce loss during high-power operation, it's not a solution for qubit coherence at single-photon levels.

questions.question: What is the typical total attenuation used on input lines from room temperature to the qubit?
questions.explanation: Typical input line attenuation is 36-50 dB total: ~20 dB at 4K, ~10 dB at intermediate stages (Still/Cold plate), and ~6-10 dB at the mixing chamber. This reduces room temperature thermal noise (n_th >> 1) to effectively zero while still allowing adequate signal power for qubit control.
